content.text:
*"Remember the former things, those of long ago; I am 
God, and there is no other."*{Isaiah 46:9}{Strong induction allows us to assume the truth of all previous cases, not just the immediate predecessor.}{Augustus De Morgan}

**Strong induction** (or complete induction) 
allows us to assume all previous cases, not just the immediate predecessor.

content.title: Strong Induction
content.content:
To prove $P(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$:

1. **Base Case(s):** Prove $P(n_0)$ (and possibly $P(n_0 + 1)$, 
 etc., as needed).

2. **Inductive Step:** Prove that for all $k \geq n_0$, if $P(j)$ 
 is true for all $n_0 \leq j \leq k$, then $P(k+1)$ is true.

content.text:
Strong induction is particularly useful when $P(k+1)$ depends on cases 
earlier than $P(k)$—for instance, in proving properties of recursive 
definitions.

content.title: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
content.problem:
Prove: Every integer $n \geq 2$ can be written as a product of primes.

proof
**Base Case:** $n = 2$ is prime, so it is trivially a product of primes.

**Inductive Step:** Assume every integer from 2 to $k$ can be written 
as a product of primes. Consider $k + 1$:

**Case 1:** $k + 1$ is prime. Then $k + 1$ is a product of primes 
(itself).

**Case 2:** $k + 1$ is composite. Then $k + 1 = ab$ where 
$2 \leq a, b < k + 1$. By the inductive hypothesis, $a$ and $b$ are 
products of primes. Therefore $k + 1 = ab$ is also a product of primes.
proof

content.title: Fibonacci inequality
content.problem:
Prove: For $n \geq 6$, the Fibonacci number $F_n \geq 2^{n/2}$.

proof
**Base Cases:** 
$F_6 = 8 \geq 2^3 = 8$. $\checkmark$
$F_7 = 13 > 2^{3.5} \approx 11.3$. $\checkmark$

**Inductive Step:** Assume true for all $j$ with $6 \leq j \leq k$ 
(where $k \geq 7$). Then:
$$
F_{k+1} &= F_k + F_{k-1} \\
&\geq 2^{k/2} + 2^{(k-1)/2} \quad \text{(by IH)} \\
&= 2^{(k-1)/2}(2^{1/2} + 1) \\
&> 2^{(k-1)/2} \cdot 2 = 2^{(k+1)/2}
$$
since $\sqrt{2} + 1 \approx 2.41 > 2$.
proof

content.title: Well-Ordering Principle
content.content: Every nonempty subset of $\N$ has a smallest element.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The well-ordering principle is equivalent to mathematical induction. 
Many proofs can be written using either method.

content.title: Well-ordering proof that $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational
content.problem:
Suppose $\sqrt{2} = a/b$ with $a, b \in \N$. Let $S = \{n \in \N : 
n\sqrt{2} \in \N\}$. Then $b \in S$, so $S \neq \emptyset$.

By well-ordering, $S$ has a smallest element $s$. Let $t = s\sqrt{2} - s 
= s(\sqrt{2} - 1)$.

Then $0 < t < s$ (since $0 < \sqrt{2} - 1 < 1$).

Also, $t\sqrt{2} = s \cdot 2 - s\sqrt{2} = 2s - t\sqrt{2}/(\sqrt{2}-1)$... 

[This leads to $t \in S$ with $t < s$, contradicting minimality.]

marginNotes.text:
Therefore, since we are surrounded by such 
a great cloud of witnesses...
